**Apply filters to SQL queries.**

**Project description**  
My organization is working to make its system more secure.  My job is to ensure the system is safe, investigate all potential security issues, and update employee computers as needed.  The following steps are examples of what I did with SQL queries and filters to perform security-related tasks.    
**Retrieve after-hours failed login attempts.**  
![][image1]The screenshot above is a query that I ran with the results in the table.  This query filters for failed login attempts that occurred after 18:00.  First, I started by SELECT \*, in which the \* represents all of the data from the log\_in\_attempts table.  Then I used a WHERE clause with an AND operator to filter my results to show only login attempts that occurred after 18:00 and were unsuccessful.  The first condition that I have in there is login\_time \> ‘18:00’, which filters for the login attempts that happened after 18:00.  The second condition is success \= FALSE, which filters for the failed login attempts.    
**Retrieve login attempts on specific dates.**  
![][image2]This screenshot shows another query that I ran, in which I was filtering for all login attempts that occurred on 2022-05-09 or 2022-05-08.  I started by selecting all data from the log\_in\_attempts table.  After that, I used a WHERE clause with an OR operator to filter my results to show only the login attempts that occurred on either 2022-05-09 or 2022-05-08.  The first condition is login\_date \= ‘2022-05-09’, which filters for that date, and the second condition is login\_date \= ‘2022-05-08’, which also filters for that date.    
**Retrieve login attempts outside of Mexico**  
**![][image3]**In this screenshot, I used a query that filtered for all login attempts that occurred in countries other than Mexico.  I started by selecting all data from the log\_in\_attempts table.  Then I used a WHERE clause with NOT to filter for countries other than Mexico.  Using the LIKE with MEX% helped filter both results, such as MEX and MEXICO.  Using the % symbol in the query helped to include both results in my filter.  The % represents any number of unspecified characters when used with LIKE.    
**Retrieve employees in Finance or Sales.**  
![][image4]In this screenshot, I used a query to return all employees in both the Finance and Sales departments.  I started by selecting all data from the employees table.  Then I used a WHERE clause with OR to filter for employees who are in the Finance and Sales departments.  I used OR instead of AND because I want all employees who are in either department.  Using the department \= Finance and department \= Sales, I was able to filter for both departments.    
**Retrieve all employees not in IT.**  
![][image5]In this screenshot, I used a query to filter for all departments besides the IT or Information Technology department.  I started by selecting all data from the employees table.  Then I used a WHERE clause with NOT to filter for employees not in the IT department.    
**Summary**  
I applied multiple filters to different SQL queries to get specific information that was requested of me.  I used two different tables; both the log\_in\_attempts and employees tables were used in my queries.  I used the AND, OR, and NOT operators to filter for the specific information that was requested of me for different tasks.  I also used the LIKE and the % to filter for certain patterns.
